story:
My childhood never stayed put. We moved so often that I stopped expecting to be remembered, stopped treating “new start” like a magic phrase. I made peace with drifting, or told myself I did.

It wasn’t always like that. When I was six and a few months into Goldwick Primary—still wearing the wrong uniform my parents dug out the night before—I got invited to Matt Corrigan’s birthday party. Dad shined my shoes, Mum combed my hair, they picked a safe gift. I was dropped off precisely eight minutes early. First mistake.

“Who are you?” his mum asked, breathless at the door, a half-inflated balloon in one hand, her curly fringe springing with each wipe of her brow. Lipstick smudged from blowing up balloons all afternoon. “I run a tight ship. You’re early, so you can help in exchange for eight minutes of free babysitting.”

Years later, turning up exactly eight minutes early to a job interview, I’d remember that line and admit my first impression had been wrong. Not a clown. Just a drill sergeant in an apron.

Kids trickled in: wet mouths, round eyes, whoops and elbows. One shock-headed blond boy dove straight between the sofa cushions like he was metal-detecting for coins or searching for himself. After the last arrival—and after Matt’s mum’s mood sweetened with whatever she splashed into her lemonade—she vanished into the kitchen, forbidding entry. “CAKE!” a dozen voices screamed. She wouldn’t confirm or deny. She would be the first to die, though we didn’t know it then. Someone would come through the back door.

“Let’s play a game!” said a girl in dungarees. “It’s Matt’s birthday, so he decides.” Matt puffed up. “Hide and Seek! I’ll count to one hundred. Go!” We scattered like marbles. I slipped into a utility room and closed the door on someone asking, “You can see me?” while his feet stuck out from behind a curtain.

There was a giant washing machine, launderette-sized. Perfect. I tugged a few of Matt’s dad’s T-shirts off the rack and stuffed them around the rim of the glass so anyone glancing in would just see a blur of fabric and slid in. One shirt advertised a beer; I mouthed its name and tagline on repeat. To this day I haven’t touched the stuff. The words wore down to mush until my breathing slowed and I could think. The door seal kissed shut behind me. I stared out my little porthole like a very serious astronaut on a very important mission.

Somewhere in the house, a bang. I shrugged it off as the TV, turned up to drown us out. Then another, then several, and then a silence so heavy it had a shape. When I pushed, the door gave at once. It had never latched properly. I climbed out, knocking the shirts loose from the glass, opened the utility-room door, and stepped into a scene I still don’t describe.

The neighbor’s teenage son, not as recovered as everyone had hoped, had slipped in through the back and opened fire. He turned the gun on himself before he reached the utility room. Maybe he’d already run out of bullets. I never learned. Either way, the machine that trapped me for those minutes also saved me.

My parents moved even more after that, as if staying still might invite danger. I told myself I got over it sooner than they did. I grew into someone who prefers mechanisms to people. I’m thirty-five now, an astronaut. I like the suit’s cocoon—tight like a hug, snug like a coffin. I like the silence, the sound of my own breath, the view through a round window while the world burns at a safe distance.

“Livingston? Are you there?” Angela from base, her voice carrying a smile with a thread of panic.

I switch the mic on. “I’m neither here nor there,” I say.

“Ha! Enough with the riddles. Thought you were hiding from me.”

I wink at the camera, wave, and turn back to Earth, where everyone is still moving, all the time.
